ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for yakitori chicken skin; closest match is chicken skin food product. Yakitori preparation (grilled with tare glaze) is not captured in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP represents grilled chicken skin as the closest defensible match; no specific yakitori tare-glazed variant exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for yakitori chicken skin or grilled chicken skin as a food concept as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of yakitori chicken skin is one skewer weighing approximately 35 g, providing around 131 kcal, 11.2 g fat, and 210 mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: Yakitori Chicken Skin — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F11020056 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this source. Saturated fat value of 9.0 g/100g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for chicken skin, cooked (NDB 05107) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; this adaptation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Yakitori chicken skin contains 8.6 g carbohydrate/100g, primarily from tare glaze (soy sauce, mirin, sugar). No direct GI measurement exists for yakitori chicken skin in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 35 (low) by analogy with mixed protein-fat-sugar dishes with similar carbohydrate density and composition (e.g., teriyaki-glazed chicken, GI ~35 per Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables). Glycemic load per 35 g serving = (35 × 8.6 × 0.35) / 100 = 1.1 g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 602 mg/100g exceeds the 600 mg/100g hypertension hard threshold; saturated fat 9.0 g/100g further contributes to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit yakitori chicken skin; sodium at 602 mg/100g independently triggers the high-concern threshold, and combined saturated fat load worsens vascular risk — if consumed, restrict to one small skewer and avoid other high-sodium foods in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 8.6 g/100g and sugar 5.0 g/100g are low, supporting glycaemic control, but high saturated fat (9.0 g/100g) worsens insulin resistance with regular consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Yakitori chicken skin has a low glycaemic index (~35) and low carbohydrate load suitable for occasional consumption in Type 2 Diabetes, but the high saturated fat content (9.0 g/100g) impairs insulin sensitivity over time — limit frequency and request minimal tare glaze to reduce sugar intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 9.0 g/100g and sodium 602 mg/100g both independently elevate cardiovascular risk through LDL cholesterol elevation and blood pressure increase respectively
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Yakitori chicken skin is high-concern for cardiovascular disease due to its saturated fat content of 9.0 g/100g raising LDL cholesterol and sodium at 602 mg/100g elevating blood pressure — individuals with CVD or CVD risk should avoid this food or limit consumption to rare occasions with strict portion control.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 9.0 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold promoting TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines; sodium 602 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g RA threshold worsening tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid yakitori chicken skin as both saturated fat (9.0 g/100g) and sodium (602 mg/100g) independently promote joint inflammation and tissue swelling — choose lean skinless chicken breast or thigh as a lower-risk alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yakitori chicken skin (焼き鳥・皮, yakitori kawa) is a popular Japanese grilled skewer dish made from chicken skin threaded onto bamboo skewers and grilled over charcoal or gas heat. It is typically glazed with tare, a sweet-savoury sauce made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar, which caramelises during grilling to produce a sticky, lacquered coating. The texture is characteristically crispy on the outside with a rich, fatty, chewy interior. The flavour profile is deeply savoury with a pronounced umami note from soy sauce, balanced by mild sweetness from mirin and sugar. Yakitori chicken skin is a staple of Japanese izakaya (gastropub) culture and is commonly served as a bar snack or appetiser alongside beer or sake. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense at 375 kcal/100g, with high fat content (32.0 g/100g, of which approximately 9.0 g is saturated fat) and moderate protein (13.3 g/100g). Sodium is elevated at 602 mg/100g due to the tare glaze, making it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Carbohydrate content is low at 8.6 g/100g with a low glycaemic index (~35), making it acceptable for occasional consumption in Type 2 Diabetes management, though the high saturated fat load warrants frequency restriction. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding soy or gluten allergens. In Singapore, yakitori chicken skin is widely available at Japanese restaurants, izakaya-style eateries, and food courts.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F11020056, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked chicken skin (NDB 05107) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat; this adaptation is documented. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed international GI tables (Atkinson et al. 2021, AJCN) as no direct GI measurement exists for yakitori chicken skin.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (9.0 g/100g) is adapted from USDA FoodData Central, cooked chicken skin entry (NDB 05107 / FNDDS), as HPB Foodo does not separately report saturated fat for this item. This is the closest approved-source equivalent and is documented per source policy. 2. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for yakitori chicken skin (tare-glazed preparation) are not reported in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2026 — limited published data exists for this traditional preparation style: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Sources consulted that confirmed unavailability: HPB Singapore Foodo (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/) and USDA FoodData Central (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/) as of April 2026. 3. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on teriyaki-glazed chicken from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables (AJCN); no direct GI measurement for yakitori chicken skin exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Halal status is uncertain — traditional yakitori uses sake and mirin (alcohol-containing ingredients); the halal flag is set to false pending confirmation of halal-certified preparation. The input record listed halal as a dietary tag but this is not confirmed for standard yakitori preparation. 5. The input record listed primary_cuisine as western and ethnic_tags as western, which is incorrect for yakitori; corrected to Japanese. 6. The input record listed plant_based as a dietary tag, which is incorrect for a chicken-based dish; removed. 7. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 entry for tare-glazed yakitori chicken skin exists as of 2024.
